chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 1/8)
chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Antisemitism Policy Trust (OSB106)**

**Public Bill Committee, Re-Committed Clauses, Online Safety Bill**

This evidence is compiled and authored by the Antisemitism Policy Trust, a charitable organisation working to educate decision makers about anti-Jewish racism. We have engaged throughout the scrutiny process for the Online Safety Bill, providing oral and written evidence to the Joint Committee on the Draft Bill, the Public Bill Committee, and associated Parliamentary Committees including the Petitions, and DCMS Select Committees.

**Overview**
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Antisemitism Policy Trust (OSB106)**

**Public Bill Committee, Re-Committed Clauses, Online Safety Bill**

This evidence is compiled and authored by the Antisemitism Policy Trust, a charitable organisation working to educate decision makers about anti-Jewish racism. We have engaged throughout the scrutiny process for the Online Safety Bill, providing oral and written evidence to the Joint Committee on the Draft Bill, the Public Bill Committee, and associated Parliamentary Committees including the Petitions, and DCMS Select Committees.

**Overview**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 2/8)
chunks.text: organisation working to educate decision makers about anti-Jewish racism. We have engaged throughout the scrutiny process for the Online Safety Bill, providing oral and written evidence to the Joint Committee on the Draft Bill, the Public Bill Committee, and associated Parliamentary Committees including the Petitions, and DCMS Select Committees. **Overview** The Trust’s position on the Bill was debated during the second day of the Bill’s report stage, when Sarah Jones MP posed a question to the Secretary of State. To clarify, the Antisemitism Policy Trust has always been supportive of the Online Safety Bill. We wish to see effective regulation of social media and other platforms online to combat, deter and address antisemitism online. We welcome the efforts of the Secretary of State to find a compromise in which various stakeholders felt they were able to have confidence that some level of action would be retained in relation to what were numerous competing concerns. That said, we are deeply disappointed that the Bill was subjected to debate about free speech, including comments from senior government Ministers and broadsheet press, which was entirely removed from the reality of what the text was set to enact. The result is that systems-level address, that is risk assessments, for legal but harmful materials is at threat of being removed from the Bill. This is an error, and we would encourage Committee members to consider amending the Bill to ensure that risk assessments form part of the package of measures Ofcom can require of a company where it is demonstrable that harm (below the legal threshold) is being caused. We are further disappointed both by the time it has taken to bring these proposals to the House, thus minimising the time available for scrutiny in the House of Lords, and that there is no opportunity for the Committee to take oral evidence from witnesses.
chunks.display_text: The Trust’s position on the Bill was debated during the second day of the Bill’s report stage, when Sarah Jones MP posed a question to the Secretary of State. To clarify, the Antisemitism Policy Trust has always been supportive of the Online Safety Bill. We wish to see effective regulation of social media and other platforms online to combat, deter and address antisemitism online. We welcome the efforts of the Secretary of State to find a compromise in which various stakeholders felt they were able to have confidence that some level of action would be retained in relation to what were numerous competing concerns. That said, we are deeply disappointed that the Bill was subjected to debate about free speech, including comments from senior government Ministers and broadsheet press, which was entirely removed from the reality of what the text was set to enact. The result is that systems-level address, that is risk assessments, for legal but harmful materials is at threat of being removed from the Bill. This is an error, and we would encourage Committee members to consider amending the Bill to ensure that risk assessments form part of the package of measures Ofcom can require of a company where it is demonstrable that harm (below the legal threshold) is being caused. We are further disappointed both by the time it has taken to bring these proposals to the House, thus minimising the time available for scrutiny in the House of Lords, and that there is no opportunity for the Committee to take oral evidence from witnesses.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 3/8)
chunks.text: legal threshold) is being caused. We are further disappointed both by the time it has taken to bring these proposals to the House, thus minimising the time available for scrutiny in the House of Lords, and that there is no opportunity for the Committee to take oral evidence from witnesses. **1\. Antisemitism Online:** Antisemitism online remains a considerable danger to Jewish people in the UK and across the world. Since the Committee last sat, Kanye West broadcast abuse of Jewish people to his 30 million Twitter followers (approximately double the world’s Jewish population) \[1\], there has been an explosion of antisemitism on the same platform \[2\] (which already hosted two antisemitic tweets for every Jew in the UK, per year \[3\] ), and for example there has been the exposition by the Antisemitism Policy Trust that videos containing racist anti-Jewish conspiracies under the #Khazarianmafira \[4\] hashtag had gathered over one million views on TikTok. \[5\] In short, antisemitism continues to proliferate online every day and action to address it remains urgent, not least because of its radicalising potential and the threat of real world consequences. **2\. Risk Assessments, the ‘Third Shield’ and Priority Content Harmful to Adults**
chunks.display_text: **1\. Antisemitism Online:** Antisemitism online remains a considerable danger to Jewish people in the UK and across the world. Since the Committee last sat, Kanye West broadcast abuse of Jewish people to his 30 million Twitter followers (approximately double the world’s Jewish population) \[1\], there has been an explosion of antisemitism on the same platform \[2\] (which already hosted two antisemitic tweets for every Jew in the UK, per year \[3\] ), and for example there has been the exposition by the Antisemitism Policy Trust that videos containing racist anti-Jewish conspiracies under the #Khazarianmafira \[4\] hashtag had gathered over one million views on TikTok. \[5\] In short, antisemitism continues to proliferate online every day and action to address it remains urgent, not least because of its radicalising potential and the threat of real world consequences. **2\. Risk Assessments, the ‘Third Shield’ and Priority Content Harmful to Adults**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 4/8)
chunks.text: gathered over one million views on TikTok. \[5\] In short, antisemitism continues to proliferate online every day and action to address it remains urgent, not least because of its radicalising potential and the threat of real world consequences. **2\. Risk Assessments, the ‘Third Shield’ and Priority Content Harmful to Adults** It is disappointing to see that a system-level address for content that is harmful to adults is being stripped from the Bill and replaced with a user-empowerment duty. The two should instead work in tandem. Rather than introducing caution at the point of design, companies will be required to introduce the most minimal amount of friction (see User Empowerment Duties in amendments 8-17) once the harm is already within the click of a user. To that end, amendments which allow for that friction to be as effective as possible, such as the opposition amendment to ensure the ‘toggle’ or ‘third shield’ is ‘on’ (a position supported at a recent Social Media APPG meeting by Conservative MP’s that are vocal proponents of free speech) should be supported by the Committee as a whole.
chunks.display_text: It is disappointing to see that a system-level address for content that is harmful to adults is being stripped from the Bill and replaced with a user-empowerment duty. The two should instead work in tandem. Rather than introducing caution at the point of design, companies will be required to introduce the most minimal amount of friction (see User Empowerment Duties in amendments 8-17) once the harm is already within the click of a user. To that end, amendments which allow for that friction to be as effective as possible, such as the opposition amendment to ensure the ‘toggle’ or ‘third shield’ is ‘on’ (a position supported at a recent Social Media APPG meeting by Conservative MP’s that are vocal proponents of free speech) should be supported by the Committee as a whole.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 5/8)
chunks.text: friction to be as effective as possible, such as the opposition amendment to ensure the ‘toggle’ or ‘third shield’ is ‘on’ (a position supported at a recent Social Media APPG meeting by Conservative MP’s that are vocal proponents of free speech) should be supported by the Committee as a whole. The removal of risk assessments takes the action related to legal but harmful content out of step with other duties in the Bill. Furthermore, in reality, it puts the onus on Ofcom and on organisations like the Antisemitism Policy Trust to reveal systems-level failures on social media and other related services – as we did in relation to Google’s so-called safe search.\[6\] Companies must consider the probability of harm and put in place effective measures, but in order to judge this, Ofcom will have to probe for information. To that end, there should be an explicit link to Ofcom’s risk profiles in this section of the Bill. This would help to ensure risk assessments are taking place as regards what an effective tool is. This would save Ofcom time and would remove the burden from organisations in the third sector. We are pleased that the materials covered by these user empowerment amendments will specifically include abuse targeting characteristics of race and religion. We also welcome the amendment seeking to put address for hateful extremism into the Bill. **3\. Enforcement of Terms of Service** The new duties (and numerous consequential amendments) relating to Terms of Service are, again, focused downstream, at the user end of engagement with a platform and not upstream in relation to design choices and programming, but we are pleased to see efforts to seek to improve the quality of the user experience. We are concerned that these provisions will however have unintended consequences.
chunks.display_text: The removal of risk assessments takes the action related to legal but harmful content out of step with other duties in the Bill. Furthermore, in reality, it puts the onus on Ofcom and on organisations like the Antisemitism Policy Trust to reveal systems-level failures on social media and other related services – as we did in relation to Google’s so-called safe search.\[6\] Companies must consider the probability of harm and put in place effective measures, but in order to judge this, Ofcom will have to probe for information. To that end, there should be an explicit link to Ofcom’s risk profiles in this section of the Bill. This would help to ensure risk assessments are taking place as regards what an effective tool is. This would save Ofcom time and would remove the burden from organisations in the third sector. We are pleased that the materials covered by these user empowerment amendments will specifically include abuse targeting characteristics of race and religion. We also welcome the amendment seeking to put address for hateful extremism into the Bill. **3\. Enforcement of Terms of Service** The new duties (and numerous consequential amendments) relating to Terms of Service are, again, focused downstream, at the user end of engagement with a platform and not upstream in relation to design choices and programming, but we are pleased to see efforts to seek to improve the quality of the user experience. We are concerned that these provisions will however have unintended consequences.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 6/8)
chunks.text: again, focused downstream, at the user end of engagement with a platform and not upstream in relation to design choices and programming, but we are pleased to see efforts to seek to improve the quality of the user experience. We are concerned that these provisions will however have unintended consequences. For example, we would be pleased to see amendments seeking to ensure Terms of Service meet a minimum standard (and reference the categories set out in the user-empowerment duties), to be set by Ofcom. In financial services, ‘Key Facts’ are required by those seeking to sell a product to meet a minimum necessary standard. If there is no minimum standard, it is possible Terms of Service will not reflect possible risks to adults on a service, will be shortened, curtailed or blunted so as to be meaningless, or may in fact lead to the removal or repeal of existing good practice. **4\. Small, High Harm Platforms and Search** Action relating both to user-empowerment and Terms of Service relates to Category 1 services. The later are to be proportionate in relation to size and capacity (not risk). For small, high harm platforms (Category 2) and for search services there is no action to be taken. New Clause 7 and Amendments 48,49 and 76-93 appears to take account of what the Trust, and a number of others, have been advocating for some time. That is, to allow Ofcom to place small, high-harm platforms in to the Category 1 status. We welcome the addition of ‘characteristics’ of a service to the list of considerations that Ofcom can assess when taking into account the status of a service. We would like confirmation during the Committee stage that this amendment would allow Ofcom to consider adding these small, high-harm platforms into Category 1.
chunks.display_text: For example, we would be pleased to see amendments seeking to ensure Terms of Service meet a minimum standard (and reference the categories set out in the user-empowerment duties), to be set by Ofcom. In financial services, ‘Key Facts’ are required by those seeking to sell a product to meet a minimum necessary standard. If there is no minimum standard, it is possible Terms of Service will not reflect possible risks to adults on a service, will be shortened, curtailed or blunted so as to be meaningless, or may in fact lead to the removal or repeal of existing good practice. **4\. Small, High Harm Platforms and Search** Action relating both to user-empowerment and Terms of Service relates to Category 1 services. The later are to be proportionate in relation to size and capacity (not risk). For small, high harm platforms (Category 2) and for search services there is no action to be taken. New Clause 7 and Amendments 48,49 and 76-93 appears to take account of what the Trust, and a number of others, have been advocating for some time. That is, to allow Ofcom to place small, high-harm platforms in to the Category 1 status. We welcome the addition of ‘characteristics’ of a service to the list of considerations that Ofcom can assess when taking into account the status of a service. We would like confirmation during the Committee stage that this amendment would allow Ofcom to consider adding these small, high-harm platforms into Category 1.

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 7/8)
chunks.text: welcome the addition of ‘characteristics’ of a service to the list of considerations that Ofcom can assess when taking into account the status of a service. We would like confirmation during the Committee stage that this amendment would allow Ofcom to consider adding these small, high-harm platforms into Category 1. User-to-user and Search Services are treated differently in the Online Safety Bill. As it stands, Google and Microsoft Bing, and voice search assistants including Amazon’s Alexa and Apple’s Siri, will be exempted from having to consider Terms of Service or introduce the user-empowerment duties. Again, we have seen antisemitic content delivered through the design of search services in recent weeks. In its report on the draft Online Safety Bill, the parliamentary joint committee recognised that search engines were not solely passive entities: "They rely on algorithmic ranking and often include automatic design features like autocomplete and voice activated searches that can steer people in the direction of content that puts them or others at risk of harm" …. "It is reasonable to expect them to come under the Bill’s requirements and, in particular, for them to conduct risk assessments of their system design to ensure it mitigates rather exacerbates risks of harm".\[7\] It is wrong to consider Search Services as inconsequential when it comes to harm. These services are private, ubiquitous, can and do direct people to harm. Failure to require proper action is a mistake. We recommend adding requirements made of Category 1 companies as regards user empowerment and Terms of Service for Search Services. **5\. Child Safety**
chunks.display_text: User-to-user and Search Services are treated differently in the Online Safety Bill. As it stands, Google and Microsoft Bing, and voice search assistants including Amazon’s Alexa and Apple’s Siri, will be exempted from having to consider Terms of Service or introduce the user-empowerment duties. Again, we have seen antisemitic content delivered through the design of search services in recent weeks. In its report on the draft Online Safety Bill, the parliamentary joint committee recognised that search engines were not solely passive entities: "They rely on algorithmic ranking and often include automatic design features like autocomplete and voice activated searches that can steer people in the direction of content that puts them or others at risk of harm" …. "It is reasonable to expect them to come under the Bill’s requirements and, in particular, for them to conduct risk assessments of their system design to ensure it mitigates rather exacerbates risks of harm".\[7\] It is wrong to consider Search Services as inconsequential when it comes to harm. These services are private, ubiquitous, can and do direct people to harm. Failure to require proper action is a mistake. We recommend adding requirements made of Category 1 companies as regards user empowerment and Terms of Service for Search Services. **5\. Child Safety**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill (Part 8/8)
chunks.text: as inconsequential when it comes to harm. These services are private, ubiquitous, can and do direct people to harm. Failure to require proper action is a mistake. We recommend adding requirements made of Category 1 companies as regards user empowerment and Terms of Service for Search Services. **5\. Child Safety** The Antisemitism Policy Trust does not have direct expertise in relation to child safety but of course children are at risk from antisemitism and antisemitic content too. Amendments like those tabled by SNP members, to ensure systems-level address in respect of children’s protections, are welcome. We do know that children are already being exposed to antisemitism online, and in some cases are using social media to spread it. It might be that the rise in antisemitic incidents in schools (against Jewish pupils and staff) including when trigger events, such as violence in the Middle East, occur are in some way inspired by social media. The Trust welcomes this opportunity to provide evidence and would be pleased to provide further information about particular amendments or the Bill as a whole. *December 2022* --- \[1\] https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-11296055/Kanye-West-posts-disturbing-tweet-claiming-hes-going-def-3-JEWISH-PEOPLE.html \[2\] https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/02/technology/twitter-hate-speech.html \[3\] https://antisemitism.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/APT-Twitter-Report-2021-Draft-4.pdf \[4\] An antisemitic conspiracy theory thriving on social media, claims that the Khazars stand behind the war in Ukraine. Khazaria existed somewhere between modern-day south-eastern Russia, southern Ukraine, Crimea and Kazakhstan. Conspiracy theorists have long claimed that the Khazars had converted to Judaism, are now living in Ukraine and want to subjugate the Russian populous. \[5\] https://mobile.twitter.com/DannyStone1/status/1590286290703949825 \[6\] https://antisemitism.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/Unsafe-Search-Report.pdf \[7\] https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/jt5802/jtselect/jtonlinesafety/129/12915.htm
